authoritative_sources.title: Strategy 6A: Open Access Scheduling for Routine and Urgent Appointments
authoritative_sources.publisher: Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AHRQ is the federal ambulatory care quality-improvement authority. Its open-access scheduling guidance directly addresses appointment access, backlog management, and same-day capacity handling in physician practices.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: demand framing, backlog review, same-day access management, and contingency scheduling

authoritative_sources.title: Strategy 6E: Rapid Referral Programs
authoritative_sources.publisher: Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AHRQ rapid-referral guidance is a direct public ambulatory workflow reference for referral-loop completion, specialist access, and handoff practices between primary and specialty care.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: referral intake normalization, referral-loop completion, and specialist handoff timing

authoritative_sources.title: Referring Practitioners Ordering Outpatient Services in Hospitals
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CMS is the primary federal authority for Medicare outpatient participation conditions and hospital outpatient ordering rules. This guidance is directly relevant because ambulatory scheduling often depends on whether a service request comes from an authorized ordering or referring practitioner.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: order and referral validation before outpatient service commitment

authoritative_sources.title: Prior Authorization for Certain Hospital Outpatient Department (OPD) Services
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CMS OPD prior authorization guidance is directly relevant to ambulatory scheduling because it names outpatient services where scheduling cannot safely ignore authorization evidence, documentation sufficiency, or review timing.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: payer dependency review and outpatient procedure booking readiness

authoritative_sources.title: Electronic Submission of Medical Documentation (esMD)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CMS esMD is the official evidence-submission path for OPD prior authorization and other documentation workflows, so it is authoritative for how supporting records move when scheduling depends on documentation clearance.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: evidence handling, submission, and documentation traceability

authoritative_sources.title: CMS Interoperability and Prior Authorization Final Rule (CMS-0057-F)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CMS-0057-F is the authoritative public baseline for provider-access retrieval and prior-authorization interoperability expectations that materially affect future-safe ambulatory scheduling and authorization visibility.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: payer retrieval, authorization visibility, and future-state system design assumptions

authoritative_sources.title: Can health care providers, such as a specialist or hospital, to whom a patient is referred for the first time, use protected health information to set up appointments or schedule surgery or other procedures without the patient's written consent?
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Department of Health & Human Services, Office for Civil Rights
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: HHS OCR is the federal privacy authority for HIPAA. This scheduling-specific FAQ directly addresses whether referred providers may use protected health information to arrange appointments and procedures without written consent.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: privacy-safe referral intake and appointment outreach

authoritative_sources.title: Notice of Privacy Practices for Protected Health Information
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Department of Health & Human Services, Office for Civil Rights
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: HHS OCR notice guidance is authoritative for first-service privacy notice handling and acknowledgment attempts, which can affect ambulatory intake and scheduling workflow for new patients scheduled by phone or electronically.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: privacy notice and first-treatment administrative readiness

authoritative_sources.title: What is a good faith estimate?
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CMS No Surprises guidance is directly relevant because uninsured and self-pay ambulatory scheduling can trigger estimate obligations that must be surfaced or routed rather than bypassed to preserve booking speed.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: self-pay and uninsured scheduling dependency review

authoritative_sources.title: Schedule - FHIR R4
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: HL7 is the recognized healthcare interoperability standards body. The FHIR Schedule resource is directly relevant to ambulatory scheduling because it models bookable availability for practitioners, rooms, and equipment.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: resource availability modeling and schedule artifact naming

authoritative_sources.title: ServiceRequest - FHIR R4
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FHIR ServiceRequest is directly relevant because many ambulatory bookings depend on a requested diagnostic, therapeutic, or referral service that must remain distinct from the schedule itself.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: service-request normalization and order-linked booking review

workflow_stages.name: Demand, order, and referral normalization
workflow_stages.description: Validate that the request is ambulatory scheduling work and normalize order, referral, visit type, modality, location, payer posture, and patient-preference facts into one scheduling frame.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Privacy, notice, and financial dependency review
workflow_stages.description: Check referral-based outreach authority, first-service privacy notice posture, self-pay estimate dependencies, and communication-channel constraints before a booking is treated as operationally safe.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Eligibility, referral, authorization, and preparation validation
workflow_stages.description: Confirm payer or self-pay posture, required referral or order state, prior authorization status, preparation instructions, and prerequisite evidence before committing a slot.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Constraint and capacity assessment
workflow_stages.description: Assess provider templates, room and equipment capacity, modality restrictions, interpreter or escort needs, location blocks, and service-level targets to determine real schedule capacity.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Recommendation-only sequencing and booking-readiness packaging
workflow_stages.description: Generate bounded timing, routing, deferral, reschedule, or waitlist recommendations using approved tie-break rules and named ambulatory artifacts.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Exception, safety, and authority escalation
workflow_stages.description: Escalate when system conflicts, clinical urgency, payer disputes, policy conflicts, or approval boundaries mean the scheduler cannot safely synthesize a deterministic answer.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

artifacts: appointment request work items, orders, referrals, and diagnosis-linked service requests
visit type matrices, duration rules, provider templates, room and equipment calendars, and overbook or waitlist rules
benefit verification, referral requirements, prior authorization status records, and payer portal notes
patient demographic, contact-preference, interpreter, accommodation, and self-pay or guarantor records
prep instructions, fasting or medication hold instructions, labs or imaging prerequisites, and consent packet status
good-faith-estimate dependency notes, privacy notice records, and outreach communication logs
escalation notes, approval-needed summaries, and schedule-readiness summaries
systems_of_record: EHR or ambulatory clinical record system
referral management or intake work queue
payer portal or clearinghouse tools for eligibility and prior authorization
document management or image repository for orders, referrals, and prep artifacts
room, equipment, and maintenance calendar system
estimate, financial counseling, or self-pay workflow system

allowed: Classify ambulatory scheduling work as in scope, out of scope, or blocked on missing prerequisites.
Assess whether available records support a bounded schedule, routing, reschedule, or allocation recommendation.
Produce evidence-backed schedule recommendations, readiness summaries, constraint logs, and escalation packages without claiming final approval authority.
Normalize ambulatory terminology such as referral, order, service request, prior auth, template, modality, and prep instructions into bounded operational outputs.
escalate: Required order, referral, authorization, preparation, room, equipment, interpreter, privacy, or tenant-policy records are missing or materially conflict.
A clinical urgency, medical-necessity, utilization-management, privacy-exception, or financial-estimate condition changes whether work may proceed.
The request requires final operational, clinical, financial, legal, or policy-override authority.
Public baseline guidance and tenant or payer policy materially conflict on direct scheduling, referral requirements, authorization handling, privacy notice timing, or self-pay workflow.
refuse: Commit final overbook, add-on clinic, staffing, payer, estimate, or operational approval.
Treat generic calendars or shift rosters as sufficient substitutes for orders, referrals, payer dependencies, preparation instructions, and room or equipment readiness evidence.
Book through missing authorization, outdated orders, unresolved system conflicts, or clinical-triage blockers to preserve utilization or patient convenience.

domain_failure_modes: The lane behaves like generic scheduling and ignores orders, referrals, payer rules, preparation instructions, and ambulatory resource constraints.
Appointments are booked before authorization, referral, or required documentation is present, creating downstream denials or unsafe arrivals.
Clinical urgency or medical-necessity questions are treated as scheduler-owned ranking decisions instead of being escalated to licensed or utilization-management owners.
Privacy, first-service notice, or self-pay estimate dependencies are skipped because they slow scheduling throughput.
Systems-of-record conflicts across EHR, practice management, payer, and maintenance tools are hidden instead of blocking unsafe commitments.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: State-specific direct-access, referral, and scope-of-service rules
unresolved_ambiguities.impact: Public federal guidance does not fully resolve when therapy, imaging, or specialty visits can be scheduled without an additional referral or order in every jurisdiction or payer contract.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Require tenant jurisdiction and payer-policy retrieval and escalate when local rule differences would change the scheduling answer.

unresolved_ambiguities.impact: CMS guidance establishes the dependency but tenants vary in whether schedulers, financial counselors, or revenue-cycle staff generate and deliver the estimate.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Treat estimate generation as adjacent-lane work unless tenant metadata explicitly delegates a narrower handoff responsibility.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Current payer interoperability adoption before January 1, 2027 compliance dates
unresolved_ambiguities.impact: CMS-0057-F defines future-state retrieval obligations, but tenant and payer API adoption before the 2027 compliance date is not uniform.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Use current tenant payer portals and manual workflows as the operational baseline while keeping API retrieval as a materialization-ready dependency.

pack_constraints: No blocking unresolved ambiguity remains for a validated spec pack because all unresolved items are converted into explicit tenant-required facts or escalation conditions.
The final pack must stay recommendation-only and must not imply final approval, clinical triage, or payer authority.
